Tom McCabe
1701 Mackinnon Avenue

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No.I-07-014
Dear Mr. McCabe:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflicts of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').r This letter is based on the
facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission,,) does not act
as the finder of fact when it renders advice. en re oglesby (1975) 1 FppC ops. 71.)
Because we do not have the necessary facts to make a determination regarding some
aspects of your question, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2
QUESTIONS
1. Do you, as a planning commissioner for the City of Encinitas, have a potential
conflict of interest that would prohibit you from partiapating in govemment decisions
regarding proposed plans to build a new park?
2. Even if you do have a conflict of interest, does the "public generally,,
exception nevertheless allow you to participate in the various decisions?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Yes. The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from participating
in governmental decisions unless an exception applies.
2. Given the limited the facts presented, we are unable to determine if the public
generally exception may apply.
I Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2,sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114 regulation 18329(c).)


File No. I-07-014
Page No. 2
are aplanrting commissioner for the City of Encinitas representing the
cardiff area' You participate in reviews ofplanningprojects in youidishict, which at
this time includes aproject in your neighboihooa to-uuita a communitypark on 44 acres
of land. The city council has already decided to purchase the land *jai*ilp 
it into a
park. The decisions before you involve environmental impact, design, and coastal
development permits for the park.
You own P{oP_erty valued at approximately $900,000 on which your residence is
located that is within 500 feet of the park. There are over 1,000 people who live within,
and hundreds ofproperties located within five hundred feet ofthe park site. you
represent Cardif{ and you state that Cardiffhas about 5,000 peopll and about 1,000
properties.
a telephone conversation we had with you, you explained that there are five
districts within Encinitas. There is one planning comirissioner appointed from each of
the five districts, and you represent the cardiff dirt i"t.
FACTS
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict_of interest provisions ensure that public officials will .,perform
their duties in an impartial manner, frie from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them.,, (Section gl00l(b).)
Section 871 00 prohibits any public official from-makin g, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence lovernmental decisions in which
the official has a financial interest, unless an exception ipplies.
The Commi::lol adopted an eight-step standard analysis to decide whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation iszooluyr)_(8).) The
general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest "iirt. whenever a public official makes
a goveflrmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests. (Section s7r03.)
Step One: Are you a public offieial?
As an appointed member of the planning commission, you are a public official
under the Act. (section 82049)3 consiquently, you maynot make, participate in
3 Section 87105 provides that when a public official who holds an office specified in section
87200 (including planning commissioners) has a conflict of interest in a decision ,roti""a ut a prrUti"
meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the it"m, oratty laentii, each tlpe
of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in
regu]ation 18702'5(b)' on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; u"a f:ii"*" the room
for the duration of the discussion andlor vote on trre liem. For closed sessiors, consent calendars, absences
and speaking as a membe-r of the public regarding personal interests, special rules foundin ,"grtution,
18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply

Page No. 3
making, or otherwise use your official position to influenc e anydecisions that will have a
reasonably foreseeable mateial financial effect on any of youieconomic interests.
(Regulation s I 87 02. I - I 87 02.4.)
step Two: wil you be making, participating in making, or using or
attempting to use your official position to influence a governmental decision?
As a member of the planning commission, you will be called upon to make
decisions regarding the environmental impactrep,orton the park, desigi, and coastal
development permits. Therefore, you wili be making, participating in making or
otherwise using your official position to influenc" u !tr"*-ental decision.
Step Three: Do you have a financial interest in the decisions at issue?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amLateial
financial effect, distinguishable fromits effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any onJ of five enumerated economic
interests. (section 87l}3;regulations ls7o3-18i03.5.) The applicable economic
interests include:
1' An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 ormore. (sectitn g7r03(a),regulation
18703'1(a).) An interest in any business entity in which u p"6ri" official is
a director, officer, parhrer, trustee, emproyee, Lr hords ,ny position or
management. (Section g7 I 03(d), regulation 1 3703. 1 (b).i
2' An interest in real property in which a public official has a direct or
indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Sectiin g7103(b), regulation
18703.2.)
3. Any source of income, including promised income, to the pubric
official that aggregates to $500 or more within r2 months prior to the
decision. (Section 871 03(c), regulation I 3703.3.)
4. Any souJ:e of gifts to the pubric official if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (section Ezi"o:1";,
regulation 18703.4.)
5' A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his ot. h",
immediate family. This is also known as the "personal financial effects,,
rule. (Section 87103, regulation 19703.5.)
Based on the facts you provided, you have an economic interest in your home in
cardiff' You state this property is valued at approximately $900,000. you tt "."ror"
have an economic interest of $2,000 or more in trr" prop"rty. (Section g7103.)


File No. I_07_014
Page No. 4
Your request for advice provided no other facts regarding any other potential
economic interests. Accordingly, our analysis is limited to your"ecoro-i" interest in the
real property upon which your residence is located.
Step Four: Is the economic interest directly involved in the governmental
decision?
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a govemmental decision if any part of the real property is within 500 feet of
the boundaries of the property that is the suLject of the goveflrmental decision.
(Regulation ls70a.2@)(1).) Yourproperty is less than ioo feet from the southwest
corner of the proposed park. You therefore have an economic interest that is directly
involved in the governmental decision.
step Five: what is the appricable materiality standard?
A conflictof interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govefllmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a)') For real property directly involved in a governmental decision, any financial
effect of the decision, even "one penny,,, is presumJd to be materirr. ln"-gJutio,
18705.z(a)(l).) This is known as the *one penny,, rule. This presumpiion"mayue
rebutted byproof that it is not reasonably foreselable that the governmental decision will
have any financial effect on the real proiert y. (Ibid.)
Accordingly, it is presumed that the planning commission,s govemmental
decisions regarding the park will have amateialfinancial effect o" io* ""oro*i"
interest in your real property, andthat you would have a conflict of interest in this
decision unless the presumption is rebutted with proof that itis not reasonably
foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have *y, "oi"""";;;;;;;,s, 
financial
effect on your property.
step six: rs the material financial effect reasonably foreseeable?
once a public official has determined the materiality standard that applies to his
orher economic interest, the next step is determining whether it is ..reaso#iy
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. An effecion economic interests is considered
"reasonably foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it wiil o".*- (Regulation
18706(a)') A financial effect need not be certain to be considered r"u.orubly foreseeable,
but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (rg75) I Fppc ops. r9g.)
whether a material financial effect is (or is not) reasonably foreseeable is necessarily a
factual question that the pubric official must uliimately decide.
Generally, each governmental decision must be analyzed independently to
determine if there will be a reasonable material financial effect on an official,s economic
interests' (In re owen (1976) 2 FPPC ops. 77.) The official must carefulf an alyze each
decision to determine the effect on the oifi.iul,. economic interests.


File No. I-07_Ot4
Page No. 5
Ultimately, whether amateial financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC ops. 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providini
advice (In re Oglesby, supra), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to ih" irrfor-ed judgmeniof the public
official.
You have stated thatyour property could 'have either a gainor loss of value.,,
You have also suggested that some decisions will have no effect-on your property, the
color of the snack bar, for example. Other decisions, however, might rr"ry *"tt have an
effect. If it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision would have eien ..o# f"rry,, effect
on your financial interest in yourproperty, then the standard applies, *d y; will have a
conflict. You must analyze each decision separately to come to a conclusion.
Step Seven: Does the.6public generally, exception apply?
Public Generally Exception
The'public-generally''exception allows an official to participate in a decision
despite a conflict of interest if the ef[ect of the decision on the official,s interest is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87\13;regulation
18707') In other words, the exception would apply iithe decision will affe-ct a significant
segment of the public in substantially the samsmanner as it would affect thepublic
official's interests.
Regulation 18707(b) provides a four-step process to determine whether the ef,[ect
of a decision is distinguishable (or not) from the effect on the public generally:
"(1) step one: Identify each specific person or real property (economic
interest) that is materially affected by the governmental decision.
"(2) Step Two: For each person or real property identified in step one,
determine the applicable 'significant segment, rule according to the
provisions of fRegulation I 3707. 1 (b)].
"(3) Step Three: Determine if the significant segment is affected by the
governmental decision as set forth in the applicable .significant seginent,
rule. If the answer is 'no,' then the analysiiLnds becauie the first p"rong of
a two-part test set forth in [Regulation 19707.|(b)l is not met, andthe
public official cannot participate in the goveflrmental decision. If the
answer is 'yes,'proceed to Step Four.
"(4) Step Four: Following the provisions of [Regulation ls7o7.l@)e)],
determine if the person or rear property iaentifieJ in step one is affected
by the govemmental decision in "substantially the.u*"^.rrur*er,, as other
persons or real property in the applicable significant segment. If the
answer is 'yes' as to each person or real property identified in Step one,

then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally and the public of,ficial miy particrpate in the decision. If
the answer i1'nol as to any per.sox or rear irop"rty iaentified in step one,
the public offic]a_l may not participate in the govemmental decision unless
one of the special rules set forth in [Regulation s 18707.2 through 1g,707.g]
applies to each person or real property triggering the conflict oiinterest.,,
(Regulation 1 87 07 (b)(t - 4).)

there are "hundreds ofproperties" within five hundred feet l,r,rr"Ii,"; *a over 1,000

governmental decision will affect a "significant segment" of the puUiic ge;lerally if it
affects ten percent olmo_re of all property olvrlers or all residential propffio*rers in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official r"p."."rrt., o, s,OOO
property owners or residential property owners in the jurisdiction of theofficials, agency.
(Regulation t 87 07 .t (b)( I XB).)

You, as a public official, must make a good faith determination as to whether the
financial effect of the decisions affect a "signiicant segment,, of the public in
"substantially the same manner" under the public genJaly exception. specifically, you
must make a good faith effort to assess w_treltrer the public generally exception applies
using some reasonable and objective method consistent wifi the uppri"uui"r"-grtuto.y
provisions. (Doi Advice Letter, No. I-04-076.) The commissiontregulations do not
impose procedural requirements mandating a particura, methoJ;f;#_;;;"g.
If the particular decision you are making affects a significant segmen! as
identified above, ofproperty owners or residenlial property owners in cardiff, the
"significant segment" prong is met. The governmentai decision need not affect a
significant segment in an "identical" manner as it affects the public official. Rather, the
decision must affect a public official's economic interest in ..substantially the same
manner" as it will affect the significant segment identified above.6 y; il;;;rt

could change for each decision. To assess whether a sign"ificant segm# *itru"

Step Eight: Does the 66legary required participation, exception apply?
Because you have not provided *{ facts to support the ..legally required,,

QUESTION: 1. Do you, as a planning commissioner for the City of Encinitas, have a potential
conflict of interest that would prohibit you from partiapating in govemment decisions
regarding proposed plans to build a new park?
2. Even if you do have a conflict of interest, does the "public generally,,
exception nevertheless allow you to participate in the various decisions?

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes. The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from participating
in governmental decisions unless an exception applies.
2. Given the limited the facts presented, we are unable to determine if the public
generally exception may apply.
